title: RAPID RESPONSE TEAM (RRT) Activation + Coordinated Bedside LEADERSHIP for an 82-Year-Old Frail Woman (Clinical Frailty Scale 6, Executed Advance Directive on File) on a Medical Floor Day 3 of Treatment for Community-Acquired Pneumonia, with a RISING NEWS/MEWS EARLY-WARNING TREND Over Hours (Tachypnea, Tachycardia, Falling SpO2, NEW CONFUSION, Soft BP) Signaling Septic Decompensation: Recognizing the TREND Before the Crash, Escalating Per Protocol Without Delay, ASSEMBLING and LEADING the RRT Bedside Until the Provider Arrives, SBAR Handoff with Closed-Loop Role Assignment, Reversing the Reversible (Oxygen, IV Fluids, Cultures + Antibiotic Re-Dose), Re-Engaging GOALS OF CARE with the Family in the Context of Frailty and Her Advance Directive (Time-Limited ICU Trial vs Continued Floor Care vs Comfort-Focused Plan), and Documenting the Episode + Team Debrief — A LEADERSHIP-FIRST Case Distinct from the Post-Operative RRT Scenarios (cases 079 and 091)
subtitle: Hospitals fail patients in two predictable ways: not noticing the EARLY-WARNING TREND, and noticing it but failing to ESCALATE. Both are leadership failures, and both are why the RAPID RESPONSE TEAM (RRT, sometimes called the Medical Emergency Team) exists: a structured way for any clinician at the bedside to summon a multi-disciplinary team when a patient is deteriorating but has not yet arrested — so the team can intervene before the code. The RRT is one of the most studied patient-safety interventions in hospital medicine; it depends on EARLY recognition, LOW-THRESHOLD activation, and EFFECTIVE coordinated leadership. EARLY WARNING SCORES (NEWS/NEWS-2, MEWS) sum points across respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, supplemental oxygen, temperature, systolic blood pressure, heart rate, and level of consciousness; a RISING score over hours — not a single deranged number — is the most useful signal in an older adult. Activation criteria commonly include any of: RR ≥ 25 or ≤ 8, HR ≥ 130 or ≤ 40, SBP ≤ 90 (or a fall of ≥ 40 from baseline), SpO2 ≤ 90% on oxygen, NEW altered mental status, decreased urine output, repeated/persistent concern by the nurse or family ('staff or family worry' is itself a legitimate criterion), threatened airway, or seizure. In OLDER ADULTS the trend matters more than the headline number — they may be tachypneic with a SpO2 still > 90, or with only NEW confusion as the first marker of sepsis ('atypical' presentation). Once activated, the RRT typically includes a critical-care MD or rapid-response APP, an ICU/respiratory therapist, and the primary RN; the BEDSIDE RN LEADS until the team arrives and then continues to anchor the bedside (the bedside RN knows the patient best). LEADERSHIP at the bedside means CLOSED-LOOP COMMUNICATION (named recipient, directed task, repeat-back), a clear SBAR HANDOFF when the team arrives, role assignment as bodies appear (one person manages airway/oxygen, one places IV access and draws labs/cultures, one runs medications/fluids, one documents, one handles family/charge nurse, one stays in clinical command), and a debrief afterward. CLINICAL response treats the reversible — high-flow oxygen, two large-bore IVs, isotonic fluids per provider, blood cultures and lactate, repeat or expand antibiotics for likely septic decompensation, electrolytes/ABG, focused exam — and watches for shock, respiratory failure, and persistent altered mental status. GOALS OF CARE are RE-ENGAGED in real time, especially for frail older adults: the Clinical Frailty Scale 6 here (moderately frail — needs help with all outside activities and many inside ones) predicts higher ICU mortality, longer length of stay, and worse functional recovery; an EXECUTED ADVANCE DIRECTIVE on file specifies her preferences. The decision pathway is not just 'ICU vs floor' but includes a TIME-LIMITED TRIAL of aggressive therapy in the ICU (e.g., 48-72 hours with defined success criteria), continued aggressive treatment on the floor with comfort emphasis if the family declines ICU, or a COMFORT-FOCUSED transition (links case-149) if continued aggressive care is not consistent with her values. The family conversation uses ASK-TELL-ASK, the SERIOUS-ILLNESS conversation framework, and shared decision-making — never an ultimatum and never a single 'yes/no on the ventilator' question. NURSING ROLE: notice the rising NEWS/MEWS TREND in an older adult and escalate per protocol (don't wait for a single deranged number); activate the RRT promptly without apology; LEAD the bedside until the team arrives and anchor it after; communicate with SBAR + closed-loop; treat the reversible while the team assembles; re-engage goals of care with the family in the context of frailty + the advance directive (time-limited trial vs floor with comfort emphasis vs comfort-focused plan); and document + debrief.

patient.name: I. Klimov
patient.code: Per advance directive on file — currently full code with explicit preference for comfort over prolonged ICU care if prognosis is poor; goal = recognize the early-warning trend before the crash, activate and LEAD the rapid response team bedside, treat the reversible while re-engaging goals of care with the family in the context of frailty and her advance directive (time-limited ICU trial vs continued floor care vs comfort-focused plan), document objectively, and debrief the team
patient.admitDate: Admitted 3 days ago to the medical floor for community-acquired pneumonia (right lower lobe consolidation on chest x-ray) with sepsis criteria at admission. Treated with IV ceftriaxone + azithromycin, IV fluids, supplemental oxygen. PMH: HFrEF (LVEF 35%), COPD GOLD II on home oxygen, CKD stage 3, mild cognitive impairment with PRESERVED CAPACITY at baseline, advanced frailty (Clinical Frailty Scale 6 — moderately frail). EXECUTED advance directive on file: 'If treatment becomes a burden without meaningful benefit, I want comfort over prolonged ICU care.' Daughter is documented DPOA-HC. Over the past 4-6 hours her NEWS score has RISEN gradually — increasing tachypnea, rising heart rate, falling oxygen saturation despite supplemental oxygen, drifting BP, and NEW confusion (baseline alert). The bedside RN recognizes the trend, escalates per protocol, and activates the rapid response team.
patient.room: Medical floor; private room; pulse oximetry continuous; cardiac telemetry; family being notified; team assembling (rapid response APP/MD, respiratory therapist, ICU charge, pharmacy on call)

vitals.spo2: 94% on 2 L NC → 88% on 4 L NC
vitals.temp: 37.4°C → 38.6°C; gradual RISING NEWS over 4-6 hours — tachypnea, tachycardia, falling SpO2 despite added oxygen, drifting BP, NEW CONFUSION (baseline alert) — this TREND is the cue, not a single deranged number; nurse escalates per protocol

38.7°C; bedside RN ACTIVATES the rapid response team without delay (does NOT wait for a single 'enough' value); calls for the charge RN; positions the patient upright; high-flow O2 in progress; family notified; RN LEADS the bedside until the team arrives — closed-loop role assignment to the responding staff

vitals.time: Team assembled + SBAR handoff + reversible treatment
vitals.temp: 38.7°C; SBAR handoff to RRT APP (Situation: 82 y/o w/ CAP on day 3 with rising NEWS + new confusion + soft BP; Background: HFrEF, COPD, CKD, CFS 6, AD on file; Assessment: likely septic decompensation; Recommendation: lactate + cultures + repeat antibiotic dose + fluid bolus + goals-of-care conversation with family); two large-bore IVs established; isotonic fluid bolus running (cautious given HFrEF, frequent reassessment); blood cultures + lactate drawn; broad-spectrum antibiotic re-dosed; ABG sent; ECG obtained

vitals.time: Re-engage GOALS OF CARE with family + advance directive review
vitals.temp: 38.5°C; family conversation using SERIOUS-ILLNESS framework + ASK-TELL-ASK + shared decision-making — review prior values + advance directive (comfort over prolonged ICU care if prognosis poor); frame the choice as TIME-LIMITED TRIAL of ICU vs continued aggressive floor care w/ comfort emphasis vs COMFORT-FOCUSED plan; daughter consults siblings; family asks for a 48-hour ICU trial w/ defined success criteria

vitals.time: Disposition + intrahospital transfer to ICU + handoff
vitals.temp: 38.4°C; decision = TIME-LIMITED 48-72-hour ICU TRIAL with defined success criteria (improving lactate, MAP > 65 off pressors within 24-48h, no progression to mechanical ventilation OR clear reversibility); RN-to-RN structured handoff using SBAR + verified patient identifiers + medication reconciliation + lines/drains/devices + family contacts + advance directive shared; transfer monitored

37.8°C; team DEBRIEF held — recognition was timely (rising NEWS trend), escalation prompt, communication clear, family supported; documentation completed (timeline + interventions + medications + family conversation + advance-directive review + team handoff); plan to reassess goals of care at 48 hours per the agreed time-limited trial

labs.n: PRESENTATION + the early-warning TREND in an older adult (recognize before the crash)
labs.v: 82 y/o woman, day 3 of inpatient treatment for community-acquired pneumonia, with a RISING NEWS over the past 4-6 hours: tachypnea (RR 22 → 28), tachycardia (HR 102 → 118), falling oxygen saturation despite added oxygen (SpO2 94% on 2 L → 88% on 4 L), drifting blood pressure (128/72 → 102/64), and NEW CONFUSION (baseline alert). The TREND is the cue — not any single deranged number; in older adults a normal SpO2 or normal BP can mask deterioration, and NEW CONFUSION may be the first or only sign of sepsis ('atypical' presentation). PMH: HFrEF (LVEF 35%), COPD on home O2, CKD3, advanced frailty (CFS 6 — moderately frail), executed advance directive on file. The leadership task at this moment: NOTICE the trend, ESCALATE per protocol without waiting for a single 'enough' value or trying to manage alone, ACTIVATE the rapid response team, LEAD the bedside until the team arrives, COMMUNICATE with SBAR and closed-loop, treat the reversible, and re-engage GOALS OF CARE with the family in the context of frailty and her advance directive — then document + debrief.
labs.r: The TREND on early warning scores (NEWS/MEWS) is the cue, not a single deranged number — especially in older adults (atypical presentations; NEW CONFUSION can be the first sepsis sign). Activation criteria include RR ≥25 or ≤8, HR ≥130 or ≤40, SBP ≤90 or drop ≥40 from baseline, SpO2 ≤90% on O2, NEW altered mental status, ↓UOP, threatened airway/seizure, AND staff or family WORRY (a legitimate trigger). RN role: recognize the trend, escalate without delay, activate RRT, do NOT manage alone.

labs.n: Rapid response team activation + structure + bedside leadership (closed-loop + SBAR)
labs.v: The RAPID RESPONSE TEAM (RRT / Medical Emergency Team) is a structured, low-threshold response for the deteriorating but not-yet-arrested patient. Typical composition: critical-care MD or rapid-response APP, ICU/RT, the primary nurse, pharmacy, and the charge RN; some teams include palliative care or chaplaincy on call. The BEDSIDE RN LEADS until the team arrives and continues to anchor the bedside (the bedside RN knows the patient best). LEADERSHIP at the bedside means CLOSED-LOOP COMMUNICATION (NAMED recipient, DIRECTED task, repeat-back, confirmation) and CLEAR ROLE ASSIGNMENT as people arrive (one manages airway/oxygen, one places IV access and draws labs/cultures, one runs medications/fluids, one documents, one handles family/charge nurse, one stays in clinical command). The HANDOFF to the responding team uses SBAR (Situation: what's happening now; Background: relevant history, medications, advance directive, baseline; Assessment: your clinical impression; Recommendation: what you're asking for) and is delivered concisely and without apology. The RN does NOT step away from the patient to manage the team alone; the RN also does NOT defer all decision-making to the responding clinician but continues to advocate as the bedside expert. DEBRIEF after the response — what went well, what to do differently, family + team support — and document the timeline objectively.
labs.r: RRT structure: critical-care MD or APP + ICU/RT + primary RN + pharmacy + charge RN (palliative/chaplaincy on call). The bedside RN LEADS until team arrives and anchors the bedside after. CLOSED-LOOP communication: named recipient + directed task + repeat-back + confirmation. ROLE assignment as people arrive (airway/O2, IV/labs, meds/fluids, documenter, family/charge, command). SBAR handoff to the responder (S-B-A-R); concise + no apology. Don't manage alone; don't defer all decision-making — advocate as bedside expert. DEBRIEF + objective documentation. RN role: lead + delegate + SBAR + debrief.

labs.n: Clinical response + goals-of-care reassessment with frailty + advance directive
labs.v: CLINICAL response treats the reversible while the team assembles: high-flow OXYGEN, TWO LARGE-BORE IVs and isotonic FLUIDS per provider (cautious in HFrEF — small boluses with frequent reassessment), BLOOD CULTURES + lactate + ABG + CBC/BMP/coagulation, broad-spectrum ANTIBIOTIC re-dose for likely septic decompensation, ECG, focused exam. Watch for SHOCK (rising lactate, persistent hypotension after fluids), RESPIRATORY FAILURE (rising work of breathing, falling SpO2 despite oxygen), and persistent ALTERED mental status. GOALS-OF-CARE re-engagement in real time is the high-yield leadership skill — especially in frail older adults: the CLINICAL FRAILTY SCALE 6 here (moderately frail — needs help with all outside activities and many inside ones) predicts higher ICU mortality, longer ICU length of stay, and worse functional recovery; the executed ADVANCE DIRECTIVE specifies her preference for comfort over prolonged ICU care if prognosis is poor. The decision pathway is NOT just 'ICU vs floor' — it includes a TIME-LIMITED TRIAL of aggressive therapy in the ICU (e.g., 48-72 hours with DEFINED success criteria — improving lactate, MAP > 65 off pressors within 24-48 hours, no progression to mechanical ventilation OR a clear reversibility), continued aggressive treatment on the floor with comfort emphasis if the family declines ICU, or a COMFORT-FOCUSED transition ([[case-149]]) if continued aggressive care is not consistent with her values. Communicate with the family using ASK-TELL-ASK, the SERIOUS-ILLNESS conversation framework, and shared decision-making — never a single 'yes/no on the ventilator' question and never an ultimatum. The bedside RN advocates for the patient's values throughout.
labs.r: Reversible treatment: high-flow O2 + 2 large-bore IVs + isotonic fluids per provider (cautious in HFrEF), cultures + lactate + ABG + labs, broad-spectrum abx re-dose, ECG, focused exam. Watch shock/respiratory failure/persistent AMS. GOALS-OF-CARE re-engagement in real time = the high-yield skill. CLINICAL FRAILTY SCALE 6 predicts higher ICU mortality/LOS + worse functional recovery; AD specifies comfort over prolonged ICU. Pathway = TIME-LIMITED TRIAL (defined success criteria) vs continued floor care w/ comfort emphasis vs COMFORT-FOCUSED ([[case-149]]). Communicate w/ ASK-TELL-ASK + serious-illness framework + shared decision-making — NOT a 'yes/no on the ventilator' ultimatum. RN advocates for patient values.

labs.n: Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors + debrief + documentation
labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct emergency practice): the RN did NOT wait for a single 'enough' value before escalating (recognized the TREND); did NOT try to manage alone or defer the call to the day-shift provider; did NOT skip SBAR or 'just do it yourself'; did NOT collapse the goals-of-care conversation into a single 'do you want the ventilator?' question; did NOT bypass the family or assume frailty alone made the decision; did NOT skip the RN-to-RN handoff or the medication reconciliation at ICU transfer; did NOT forget to document timeline + interventions + medications + family conversation + advance-directive review; and did NOT skip the team debrief. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: timely recognition of the rising NEWS trend; prompt RRT activation without delay; the bedside RN LEADING until the team arrived and anchoring the bedside after; closed-loop role assignment; clear SBAR handoff to the responding APP; reversible treatment (O2, fluids cautious in HFrEF, cultures, antibiotic re-dose); real-time goals-of-care re-engagement using the serious-illness framework with the family; an option-rich decision (time-limited ICU trial vs continued floor care with comfort emphasis vs comfort-focused plan); a structured RN-to-RN handoff at ICU transfer with medication reconciliation and AD/POLST transmission; objective documentation; and a team debrief afterward. EXPECTED outcome: the deterioration is recognized and treated early, the family is supported in a values-aligned decision, and either the patient improves on a time-limited ICU trial or transitions to a comfort-focused plan — but in either case her preferences guide the path.
labs.r: Negatives: didn't wait for a single 'enough' value, didn't manage alone, didn't skip SBAR, didn't collapse goals to 'ventilator yes/no,' didn't bypass family, didn't skip RN-to-RN handoff + med rec, didn't skip documentation/debrief. Protective: timely trend recognition + prompt RRT activation + RN leading + closed-loop roles + SBAR + reversible treatment + real-time goals-of-care reengagement w/ serious-illness framework + option-rich decision (time-limited ICU trial vs floor w/ comfort vs comfort-focused) + structured RN-to-RN handoff + med rec + AD/POLST + documentation + debrief. RN role: trend recognition + leadership + advocacy.

labs.v: 3.6 mmol/L (elevated — sepsis with tissue hypoperfusion; trend with serial values during time-limited trial)
9 (red zone — urgent threshold reached after gradual rise from 3 → 5 → 7 → 9 over 4-6 hours; the TREND is more meaningful than the single value)
labs.r: 0-4 low risk; 5-6 medium; ≥7 high — urgent response

labs.v: 6 (moderately frail — needs help with all outside activities and many inside ones; predicts higher ICU mortality, longer ICU LOS, and worse functional recovery — factor into goals-of-care reassessment)
labs.r: 1 very fit; 5 mildly frail; 6 moderately; 7 severely; 9 terminally ill

labs.v: 16.8 x10^9/L with left shift (worsening septic response despite antibiotics — re-dose and broaden per cultures)

nursesNote: Medical floor day 3 of CAP — 82 y/o woman, CFS 6, AD specifies comfort over prolonged ICU care if prognosis poor, daughter = DPOA-HC. Over 4-6 hours NEWS rose 3 → 5 → 7 → 9 (RR 22 → 28, HR 102 → 118, SpO2 94% on 2 L → 88% on 4 L, BP 128/72 → 102/64, NEW confusion). Recognized the TREND (not a single 'enough' value), escalated per protocol WITHOUT DELAY, ACTIVATED the RRT (did NOT try to manage alone), called for charge RN, positioned upright, high-flow O2, family notified. RN LED the bedside until team arrived w/ CLOSED-LOOP role assignment. SBAR handoff to RRT APP: S = 82F day 3 CAP, rising NEWS, new confusion, soft BP; B = HFrEF/COPD/CKD/CFS 6/AD on file; A = likely septic decompensation; R = lactate + cultures + abx re-dose + careful fluids + goals-of-care conversation. Reversible treatment in parallel: 2 large-bore IVs; cautious isotonic fluids in HFrEF w/ frequent reassessment; blood cultures + lactate (3.6) + ABG + CBC (WBC 16.8 w/ left shift) drawn; broad-spectrum abx re-dosed; ECG; focused exam. Real-time GOALS-OF-CARE re-engagement w/ family using SERIOUS-ILLNESS framework + ASK-TELL-ASK + shared decision-making — reviewed prior values + AD; framed as option-rich (TIME-LIMITED 48-72h ICU TRIAL w/ defined success criteria vs continued aggressive floor care w/ comfort emphasis vs COMFORT-FOCUSED plan — [[case-149]]); daughter consulted siblings; family chose 48-72h TIME-LIMITED TRIAL w/ defined success (improving lactate, MAP > 65 off pressors within 24-48h, no progression to mech vent OR clear reversibility); reassessment at 48h. RN-to-RN structured handoff to ICU using SBAR + 2 identifiers + medication reconciliation + lines/drains/devices + family contacts + AD/POLST transmission. DEBRIEF: recognition timely (trend, not headline number), escalation prompt, communication clear, family supported, advocate at bedside throughout. Objective DOCUMENTATION: timeline + interventions + medications + family conversation + AD review + ICU handoff. NEGATIVES (correct practice): didn't wait for a single 'enough' value, didn't manage alone, didn't skip SBAR, didn't collapse goals to 'ventilator yes/no,' didn't bypass family, didn't skip RN-to-RN handoff + med rec, didn't skip debrief/documentation.
providerOrders: EARLY-WARNING / ESCALATION: bedside RN to escalate per facility protocol whenever the NEWS/MEWS rises significantly or any single criterion is met (RR ≥ 25 or ≤ 8, HR ≥ 130 or ≤ 40, SBP ≤ 90 or drop ≥ 40 from baseline, SpO2 ≤ 90% on oxygen, new altered mental status, decreased urine output, threatened airway/seizure, or staff/family worry); do NOT wait for a single 'enough' value or try to manage alone
RRT ACTIVATION: activate the rapid response team for the criteria above; charge RN notified; bedside RN leads until the team arrives and continues to anchor the bedside afterward

BEDSIDE LEADERSHIP: closed-loop communication (named recipient, directed task, repeat-back); role assignment as people arrive (airway/oxygen, IV access + labs/cultures, medications/fluids, documenter, family/charge, clinical command); SBAR handoff to the responding APP/MD (Situation/Background/Assessment/Recommendation)

REVERSIBLE TREATMENT: high-flow oxygen titrated to SpO2 target; two large-bore IVs; isotonic crystalloid bolus per provider (cautious in HFrEF — small boluses with frequent reassessment); blood cultures + lactate + ABG + CBC/BMP/coagulation; re-dose or broaden the antibiotic for likely septic decompensation per protocol; ECG; focused exam; consider noninvasive ventilation per provider

GOALS OF CARE: re-engage with the family in real time using the serious-illness conversation framework + ask-tell-ask + shared decision-making; review the patient's executed advance directive and Clinical Frailty Scale; frame the choice as option-rich (time-limited 48-72-hour ICU trial with defined success criteria vs continued aggressive floor care with comfort emphasis vs comfort-focused plan); document the decision and rationale

DISPOSITION: time-limited 48-72-hour ICU trial with defined success criteria (improving lactate, MAP > 65 off pressors within 24-48 hours, no progression to mechanical ventilation OR a clear reversibility); RN-to-RN structured handoff to ICU using SBAR + two identifiers + medication reconciliation + lines/drains/devices + family contacts + advance directive/POLST transmission

REASSESSMENT: re-evaluate at 48 hours per the agreed trial criteria; if not improving, re-engage goals-of-care for comfort-focused transition (links case-149); if improving, continue and plan step-down + post-ICU care

DEBRIEF + DOCUMENTATION: lead a team debrief after the response (what went well, what to do differently, family + team support); document the timeline, interventions, medications, family conversation, advance-directive review, and ICU handoff objectively

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at this acute deterioration?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: The RISING NEWS over hours (RR up, HR up, SpO2 falling despite added oxygen, BP drifting down, NEW confusion) is the cue — the TREND, not a single deranged number, especially in an older adult
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — trend recognition is the leadership skill that prevents the code. RN role: recognize the trend.

opts.text: Activation criteria are met multiple ways (RR ≥ 25, NEW altered mental status, SpO2 ≤ 90% on oxygen, soft BP) — and 'staff or family worry' is itself a legitimate criterion
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the threshold is low and many doors are open. RN role: activate without apology.

opts.text: The RRT structure (critical-care MD/APP + ICU/RT + primary RN + pharmacy + charge RN) responds to the deteriorating but not-yet-arrested patient; the bedside RN LEADS until the team arrives and anchors the bedside after
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the RN does not step aside or try to manage alone. RN role: bedside leadership.

opts.text: Reversible treatment (high-flow oxygen, two large-bore IVs, cautious fluids in HFrEF, cultures, antibiotic re-dose, lactate, ABG, ECG) happens in parallel with team assembly
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — buy time while the team mobilizes. RN role: treat the reversible.

opts.text: Goals of care must be re-engaged in real time with the family in the context of frailty (CFS 6) and the patient's executed advance directive — using the serious-illness framework, not a 'yes/no on the ventilator' ultimatum
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the right destination depends on the patient's values. RN role: advocate + facilitate.

opts.text: The disposition can be a TIME-LIMITED ICU TRIAL with defined success criteria, continued aggressive floor care with comfort emphasis, or a comfort-focused plan — and is followed by a structured RN-to-RN handoff with medication reconciliation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — option-rich pathway and safe handoff. RN role: shared decision + structured handoff.

opts.text: Wait for a single 'enough' value (e.g., SpO2 below a hard cutoff) before activating the RRT, and try to manage the deterioration alone in the meantime
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The RISING TREND is the cue — waiting for a single deranged number costs minutes that matter, and managing alone deprives the patient of the team.

opts.text: Collapse the goals-of-care conversation into a single 'do you want her on a ventilator, yes or no?' question for the daughter to answer immediately
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. A serious-illness conversation uses ask-tell-ask and shared decision-making about VALUES and goals, not an ultimatum on a single intervention.

opts.text: Early warning scores (NEWS/MEWS) sum points across RR, SpO2, supplemental O2, temperature, SBP, HR, and level of consciousness; a rising score over hours is the most useful signal — especially in older adults
opts.rat: CORRECT — early-warning principle. Foundation for recognition.

opts.text: RRT activation criteria are intentionally LOW-THRESHOLD and include NEW altered mental status and 'staff or family worry' as legitimate triggers
opts.rat: CORRECT — activation principle. Foundation for prompt escalation.

opts.text: The bedside RN LEADS the response until the team arrives and continues to anchor the bedside afterward; the RN advocates as the bedside expert and does not defer all decision-making
opts.rat: CORRECT — leadership principle. Foundation for safe response.

opts.text: Closed-loop communication (named recipient, directed task, repeat-back, confirmation) and a clear SBAR handoff are the operational tools of the response
opts.rat: CORRECT — communication principle. Foundation for coordination.

opts.text: Frailty (e.g., Clinical Frailty Scale 6) predicts higher ICU mortality, longer LOS, and worse functional recovery — and is factored into the goals-of-care reassessment, never as the sole decision
opts.rat: CORRECT — frailty principle. Foundation for shared decision-making.

opts.text: The decision pathway is option-rich: a time-limited ICU trial with defined success criteria, continued aggressive floor care with comfort emphasis, or a comfort-focused plan
opts.rat: CORRECT — decision-pathway principle. Foundation for matching care to values.

opts.text: Once the RRT is called, the bedside RN should step away from the patient and let the responding team take over completely
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The bedside RN remains the patient's anchor and clinical expert; the RN continues to assess, advocate, and document.

opts.text: Frailty alone (e.g., CFS 6) is sufficient justification for unilateral comfort-focused transition without re-engaging the family or reviewing the advance directive
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Frailty informs the conversation; it does not replace the patient's values, the surrogate, or the advance directive.

opts.text: Recognize the rising NEWS trend and ACTIVATE the rapid response team without delay — do not manage alone and do not wait for a single 'enough' value
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. The activation IS the leadership act that prevents the code. RN role: recognize + activate.

opts.text: LEAD the bedside until the team arrives — closed-loop role assignment, positioning, high-flow oxygen, IV access, family notification
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. The bedside RN runs the bedside until the team is present. RN role: lead + delegate.

opts.text: Hand off using SBAR to the responding clinician and treat the reversible (oxygen, cautious fluids, cultures, antibiotic re-dose, lactate, ABG, ECG)
opts.rat: #3 — Pass the baton and treat what can be reversed. RN role: SBAR + reversible treatment.

opts.text: Re-engage GOALS OF CARE with the family in real time using the serious-illness framework + ask-tell-ask + shared decision-making — anchored in the advance directive and frailty context
opts.rat: #4 — The destination depends on the patient's values; this is the high-yield decision-making step. RN role: facilitate + advocate.

opts.text: Make the disposition decision (time-limited ICU trial vs floor with comfort emphasis vs comfort-focused), execute a structured RN-to-RN handoff, document objectively, and lead a team debrief
opts.rat: #5 — Close the loop with a safe handoff and a debrief. RN role: disposition + handoff + documentation + debrief.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this rapid response.

opts.text: Recognize the rising NEWS trend and activate the RRT without delay; notify the charge RN; do not manage alone
opts.rat: Trend recognition + low-threshold activation are the leadership acts that prevent the code. RN role: recognize + activate.

opts.text: Lead the bedside with closed-loop role assignment (airway/oxygen, IV access + cultures/labs, medications/fluids, documenter, family/charge, clinical command)
opts.rat: Coordinated bedside leadership. RN role: lead + delegate.

opts.text: Hand off using SBAR to the responding APP/MD and treat the reversible (high-flow oxygen, cautious fluids in HFrEF, cultures, antibiotic re-dose, lactate, ABG, ECG)
opts.rat: Pass the baton and treat what can be reversed. RN role: SBAR + reversible treatment.

opts.text: Re-engage goals of care with the family using the serious-illness framework + ask-tell-ask + shared decision-making, anchored in the advance directive and Clinical Frailty Scale
opts.rat: The destination depends on the patient's values. RN role: facilitate + advocate.

opts.text: Offer an option-rich decision pathway: time-limited ICU trial with defined success criteria, continued aggressive floor care with comfort emphasis, or a comfort-focused plan
opts.rat: Matches care to values. RN role: option-rich decision.

opts.text: Execute a structured RN-to-RN handoff at ICU transfer using SBAR + two identifiers + medication reconciliation + lines/drains/devices + family contacts + AD/POLST transmission; document objectively and lead a team debrief
opts.rat: Safe handoff and closed-loop response. RN role: handoff + documentation + debrief.

opts.text: Wait for a single 'enough' value (e.g., SpO2 below a hard cutoff) before activating the RRT, and try to manage the deterioration alone in the meantime
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the rising trend is the cue; waiting costs minutes that matter, and managing alone deprives the patient of the team.

opts.text: Step away from the patient as soon as the team arrives and defer all decision-making to the responding APP without further bedside advocacy
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the bedside RN remains the patient's anchor and clinical expert; continued advocacy is part of the response.

opts.text: Collapse the goals-of-care conversation into 'do you want her on a ventilator, yes or no?' and demand an immediate answer from the daughter
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — a serious-illness conversation is about values and goals using ask-tell-ask and shared decision-making, not an ultimatum on a single intervention.

opts.text: Treat frailty (CFS 6) alone as sufficient justification for a unilateral comfort-focused transition without re-engaging the family or reviewing the advance directive
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — frailty informs the conversation; it does not replace the patient's values, the surrogate, or the advance directive.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Recognize the rising NEWS trend and ACTIVATE the rapid response team without delay; notify the charge RN
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. The activation IS the leadership act that prevents the code. RN role: recognize + activate.

opts.text: Lead the bedside with closed-loop role assignment; high-flow oxygen; IV access; family notification; continuous assessment
opts.rat: #2 — Second. The bedside RN runs the bedside until the team is present. RN role: lead + delegate.

opts.text: Hand off using SBAR to the responding APP/MD and treat the reversible (cautious fluids in HFrEF, cultures, antibiotic re-dose, lactate, ABG, ECG)
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Pass the baton and treat what can be reversed. RN role: SBAR + reversible treatment.

opts.text: Re-engage goals of care with the family using the serious-illness framework + ask-tell-ask + shared decision-making — anchored in the advance directive and frailty context — and offer an option-rich decision pathway
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. The destination depends on the patient's values. RN role: facilitate + advocate.

opts.text: Execute a structured RN-to-RN handoff at disposition, document objectively, and lead a team debrief
opts.rat: #5 — Closing. Safe handoff + documentation + debrief complete the response. RN role: handoff + documentation + debrief.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action/outcome, classify as APPROPRIATE or INAPPROPRIATE.

opts.text: The RN recognized the rising NEWS trend over hours, activated the RRT without delay, led the bedside with closed-loop role assignment, and handed off using SBAR
opts.rat: Trend recognition + low-threshold activation + bedside leadership + SBAR. Model practice.

opts.text: Reversible treatment was delivered in parallel — high-flow oxygen, cautious fluids in HFrEF, cultures and lactate drawn, antibiotic re-dosed, ABG and ECG obtained — while the team assembled
opts.rat: Treat the reversible while the team mobilizes. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Goals of care were re-engaged with the family using the serious-illness framework + ask-tell-ask + shared decision-making, anchored in the advance directive and Clinical Frailty Scale, and an option-rich pathway was offered (time-limited ICU trial / floor with comfort emphasis / comfort-focused)
opts.rat: Patient values drive the decision. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: A structured RN-to-RN handoff at ICU transfer included SBAR + two identifiers + medication reconciliation + lines/drains/devices + family contacts + AD/POLST transmission; the team debriefed afterward
opts.rat: Safe handoff and closed-loop response. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Alternative — the RN waited for a single 'enough' value before activating the RRT and tried to manage the deterioration alone; the patient progressed to cardiac arrest before the team arrived
opts.rat: ⚠️ Waiting for a single deranged number and managing alone is the classic leadership failure that turns a deterioration into a code.

opts.text: Alternative — the goals-of-care conversation was collapsed into 'do you want her on a ventilator, yes or no?' and the daughter was pressured to answer immediately; the family was traumatized
opts.rat: ⚠️ A serious-illness conversation is about values + goals using ask-tell-ask + shared decision-making, not an ultimatum on a single intervention.

opts.text: Alternative — frailty (CFS 6) alone was treated as sufficient justification for a unilateral comfort-focused transition without re-engaging the family or reviewing the advance directive
opts.rat: ⚠️ Frailty informs the conversation; it does not replace the patient's values, the surrogate, or the advance directive.

opts.text: Alternative — the bedside RN stepped away as soon as the team arrived and deferred all decision-making without further bedside advocacy; documentation was incomplete and the patient's values were not represented
opts.rat: ⚠️ The bedside RN remains the patient's anchor and clinical expert; continued advocacy is part of the response.

opts.text: Alternative — handoff to the ICU was verbal only without SBAR, two identifiers, medication reconciliation, or advance-directive transmission; a critical medication was missed at ICU arrival
opts.rat: ⚠️ Verbal-only handoffs without structure are the most common transition failure. Use SBAR + identifiers + medication reconciliation + AD/POLST.

opts.text: On reassessment at 48 hours, the time-limited ICU trial showed clear improvement (lactate trending down, MAP > 65 off pressors, no progression to mechanical ventilation); plan continued with step-down + post-ICU care
opts.rat: Time-limited trial with defined success criteria followed and acted on. Ideal trajectory.
